## Experimental administrative population census: Data sources, methods, and quality (second iteration)
19 October 2022, 12:00pm
Experimental administrative population census: Data sources, methods, and quality describes an experimental approach for deriving census information from administrative (admin) data. This paper discusses the data and methods used to compile the second iteration of the experimental administrative population census (APC) covering the period 2006–2021 and provides information about the quality of the data.
The first iteration of the APC , released in August 2021, was an annual series from 2006 to 2020, focusing on demographic and identity variables: age, sex, geography, ethnicity, Māori descent, birthplace, and years since arrival in New Zealand. This second iteration continues the time series to the 2021 year, and includes some changes informed by feedback received. The APC now includes 19 census variables, with additional information on education, income, and work topics, and for the variables number of children born and address n years ago. Another iteration is planned in 2023 and feedback from data users will help guide further development.
Download the document below or read the summary and Appendix 5: Information by variable online.
- Experimental administrative population census: Data sources, methods, and quality (second iteration) Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 827 KB

### Experimental administrative population census: Data sources, methods, and quality (second iteration)
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 827 KB
The experimental administrative population census (APC) data are NOT official statistics. Rather, they are published as an experimental output from research using a different methodology than what is currently used to produce the census or official population statistics.
While the core information obtained through the census is similar around the world, the way census information is obtained is adapted to the circumstances of each country, and methods have evolved over time. The APC is part of Stats NZ’s census transformation programme looking at the potential for a future census based on administrative data supported by sample surveys. Administrative data is data already collected by government agencies or private organisations while conducting their business or services. Examples include birth registrations and tax records. The APC derives census-type information from linked administrative sources available in Stats NZ’s Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI). The APC has been approved as an IDI research project under the Ngā Tikanga Paihere framework.
With the release of the APC, Stats NZ aims to demonstrate the information available currently and to provide a focus for discussion about the benefits and limitations associated with census information based on administrative sources. We are seeking feedback from everyone, including government agencies, local government, iwi and Māori organisations, academics, and the general public.
The research and development programme into future census models is designed to be iterative. The APC builds on experimental population estimates derived from linked administrative data released in 2016, 2017, and 2018, and extensive and detailed research on the quality of administrative data for census variables. The APC is based on the admin New Zealand resident population and supplements this with characteristics of individuals derived from administrative data.
Outputs are released on the Stats NZ website as data tables supported by a graphical and mapping interface: experimental administrative population census . All outputs have been confidentialised, and de-identified APC unit-record data is also available in the IDI.

## Appendix 5: Information by variable
This section provides details for each of the APC's variables.
- Usual residence address
- Usual residence n years ago indicator
- Number of children born alive
- Years since arrival in NZ
- Highest qualifications and field of study
- Personal income: Total personal income and income sources
- Employment indicator and status in employment
- Industry and sector of ownership
- Appendix 5A: Data tables
The APC uses age and sex from the IDI personal details table.
The APC very closely follows the national ERP age distribution in 2018, as shown in figure 1. The APC counts are closer to the ERP than the 2018 Census usual resident population counts.
NZ usual resident population by age, APC and ERP at 30 June 2018, and 2018 Census
To understand the small differences visible in figure 1, figure 2 shows the percent differences between the APC and the ERP by 5-year age groups and sex in 2018.
Recent analysis suggests that the 2013 ERP age distribution may have too few males aged 15 to 59 years, partially due to biases in the imputation of age and sex for substitute records in the 2013 Census (Stats NZ, 2022). Therefore, comparisons with the 2013 ERP may misrepresent the accuracy of the APC population, and comparisons against the ERP from 2018 may be more realistic.
The 2018 APC resident population meets the quality standard requirement of all 5-year age group by sex estimates being within 5 percent of the benchmark value, but does not achieve the requirement of 90 percent within 1.5 percent. This is a good result considering that there has been no coverage adjustment for the APC population.
Further details for the time series are available in the first APC report (Stats NZ, 2021).

## Usual residence address
The most recently updated administrative address prior to the reference date is selected as the usual residence address. However, certain administrative sources were deemed to be of lower quality, and therefore only used when other address information was not available. See Stats NZ (2017) for further details. 2013 Census data is used only when no high-quality administrative address is available.
Recent improvements (March 2019) in address-matching in the IDI have increased the accuracy of address and geography variables over previously reported results.
Subnational estimates depend on both the population included in the APC, and the availability and accuracy of address information. Coverage for address is very high, ranging from 97 percent in 2006 to above 99 percent from 2013 onwards.
The quality rating score for usual residence is derived by comparing the consistency of the APC usual residence with usual residence in the 2018 Census for meshblocks. Consistency varies depending on the geographic level. When comparing usual residence address ID, 83 percent of the APC addresses are the same as those in the census, and 86 percent agree for meshblocks. Consistency increases for larger geographic areas, reaching 97 percent for regional council.
Agreement between APC in 2018 and 2018 Census (proportion)
Territorial authority
From figure 3 below it can be seen that consistency is lowest for young adults, in particular the 20 to 24 years age group. A similar pattern is seen at all geographic levels, though it is less pronounced for the larger TA and regional council geographies.
Address consistency, between APC in 2018 and 2018 Census, by five-year age group
Age (five-years age groups)
Territorial authority
Comparing the usual residence counts from the APC for Territorial authority/Auckland local boards (TALB) and SA2 to the ERP we see decreasing variability in the differences between the two sources at each successive census year, and a general shift towards APC undercount by 2018. Experimental administrative population census provides more detail on this shift (Stats NZ, 2021).
The 2018 APC TALB total populations do not meet the quality standard accuracy requirement of all being within ±2.5 percent for TALB of 100,000 or more people (67 percent meet the standard), and all within ±5 percent for TALB with fewer than 100,000 people (85 percent meet the standard; table A2 in appendix 3 provides more detail). Age groups within TALB however do meet, or are close to meeting, the less stringent accuracy requirements for age groups.
The 2018 results are close to fully meeting the quality standard for the SA2 geography, with 83 percent of SA2s within 5 percent (the standard is at least 80 percent), and 96 percent within 10 percent (where the standard asks for all SA2s to be within 10 percent). The SA2 quality standards are wider than for larger areas because in the past it has been more difficult for the methods used in the ERP to estimate populations for small areas in between censuses.
In order to meet the accuracy required for official population statistics, coverage adjustment will need to account for variation in coverage across TALB, which will be due to some misclassification of administrative address information in addition to net undercount of the total population.

## Usual residence n years ago indicator
The statistical standard for usual residence n years ago indicator gives the definition as “a respondent’s usual residence n years ago in relation to their usual residence on the date that the data is collected”.
In the APC we provide the indicators for the usual residence address 1 year ago and 5 years ago in relation to the address on 30 June of the reference year. The indicator identifies whether a person’s usual address has remained the same or has changed in that time. The classification allows for those who were not a member of the New Zealand resident population n years ago, either because they were not born, or were overseas n years ago. If this cannot be determined, the information is missing. We note that a usual residence of ‘no fixed abode’ cannot be determined from current admin sources.
Usual residence 1 year ago is derived for the APC from 2007, and usual residence 5 years ago from 2011.
The usual residence n years ago indicator is derived from the APC usual residence address in the reference year and previous year, APC age, and Customs border movements data. The subject population is the APC resident population in the reference year.
APC usual residence n years ago indicator derivation
Derived from APC usual residence address and age variables, and Customs border movements.
For every unique individual (snz_uid) in the APC in the reference year, for n = 1 and n = 5:
- If the individual is present in the APC n years ago: if the address n year ago matches the usual residence address, classify as ‘same as usual residence’ if the address n year ago does not match the usual residence address, classify as ‘elsewhere in New Zealand’ if the address n years ago is missing, classify as ‘New Zealand not further defined’.
- if the address n year ago matches the usual residence address, classify as ‘same as usual residence’
- if the address n year ago does not match the usual residence address, classify as ‘elsewhere in New Zealand’
- if the address n years ago is missing, classify as ‘New Zealand not further defined’.
- If the individual is not present in the APC n years ago, use the appropriate classification: if aged under n years, then ‘not born n years ago’ if not present in New Zealand according to border movements data, then ‘overseas n years ago’ otherwise ‘missing’.
- if aged under n years, then ‘not born n years ago’
- if not present in New Zealand according to border movements data, then ‘overseas n years ago’
- otherwise ‘missing’.
Coverage for the usual residence n years ago indicator reflects the coverage of the address data for the relevant year. Coverage is high throughout the APC time series and continues to improve, from 96 percent in 2007 (1 year ago) and 2011 (5 years ago) respectively, to 99 percent in 2021.
The quality rating score for the usual residence n years ago indicator is derived by comparing the consistency between individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. Consistency for the usual residence n years ago indicator is defined as exact agreement between responses at address level. Consistency is 0.87 for the 5 years ago indicator, but lower at 0.83 for the 1 year ago indicator.
In the 2018 Census, usual residence 5 years ago was derived by matching back to the 2013 Census usual residence, and is unusual in that there is no question on the census form. Since the admin derivation is the same for both indicators, a possible explanation for the 4 percent drop in consistency for usual residence 1 year ago is how respondents have answered the census question.
We compare distributions between the APC in 2018 and the 2018 Census for the usual residence 1 year and 5 years ago indicators (figure 4). Results are shown for the most detailed address level. Similar distributions can be easily created for SA1 or higher geographies.
The usual residence 5 years ago indicator shows a very similar distribution for both sources, with all categories within 2 percentage points. For usual residence 1 year ago indicator people were more likely (by about 6 percent) to say they live at the same address in the census than when derived in the APC, and correspondingly less likely to be ‘elsewhere in New Zealand’. Again, a possible explanation is that the address 5 years ago indicator derived from matching back to the 2013 Census is more reliable than the information from the 1 year ago question on the census form.
The longitudinal nature of the APC data makes it straightforward to derive gross and net flows between geographic areas. While we have not examined these results in detail, APC output tables on the website have been created for net flows between TALB.

## Number of children born alive
The statistical standard for fertility defines the number of children born alive as “the number of children ever born alive to each woman resident in New Zealand, aged 15 years and over. Foetal deaths and stillborn children are excluded, as are stepchildren, adopted children, foster children, and wards of the state.” The standard classification Number of children born v1.0.0 has non-residual categories for ‘no children’, ‘one child’, to ‘nine children’, and ‘ten or more children.’
Number of children born alive is derived from DIA birth registrations, 2013 Census responses, and Customs border movements.
Each birth registration record includes information about the child born and the child’s parents. Stillbirths are excluded, however DIA birth registrations in the IDI include entries for adopted children without an indicator. We use this parent-child information to count the number of children born to a woman up to the reference date (June 30 of the APC year).
The mother’s information in the birth registration must be linked to their IDI spine identity. Linkage rates of parents to the IDI spine are very high for births registered from 1985 onwards, the earliest year in which birth registrations are fully digitised. From 1972, when the date of birth for parents was first recorded, fewer than half the parent records are linked to the IDI spine, and none before 1972. Therefore, DIA birth registrations provide a close to complete record of children born in New Zealand to mothers aged 15 or over from 1985 on, that is, mothers born since 1970. Therefore, to avoid undercounting the number of children to older women, for the APC outputs we only use the DIA birth registrations for women born since 1970.
Birth registrations provide positive identification of births in New Zealand, but do not include children born overseas, and do not identify those with no children. The 2013 Census helps to fill these gaps. The 2013 Census question on number of children born alive is used to provide information for: those with no children in 2013, historic data for children born in New Zealand before the mother’s record can be reliably linked to the IDI spine, and children born overseas before the 2013 Census. The 2013 Census does not provide a date for when children were born.
The value for number of children up to March 2013 from DIA is given preference over 2013 Census except when the 2013 Census has a higher value than the DIA.
We also derive a value of ‘No children’ in certain cases, assuming that the absence of a New Zealand birth registration and a limited amount of time spent outside of New Zealand means that the woman has not had a child. This applies to those who are not present in the 2013 Census and were aged under 15 in 2000 (when Customs border movements are first available).
APC number of children born derivation
Derived from DIA birth registrations, the 2013 Census, and Customs border movements
For every individual (snz_uid) female 15 years or older in the APC in the reference year:
- Count the number of children associated with each mother in the DIA birth registrations up to June 30 of each reference year: exclude DIA births for mothers born before 1970 for APC outputs.
- exclude DIA births for mothers born before 1970 for APC outputs.
- Obtain the number of children born as recorded in the 2013 Census: if the 2013 Census has a valid value (including No children) and there are no DIA births recorded up to the census date, then assign the census value for years 2006 to 2013 if the census value is higher than DIA at the census date then add the difference to the DIA count for years from 2013 if the census value is lower than DIA at the census date, then discard the census count.
- if the 2013 Census has a valid value (including No children) and there are no DIA births recorded up to the census date, then assign the census value for years 2006 to 2013
- if the census value is higher than DIA at the census date then add the difference to the DIA count for years from 2013
- if the census value is lower than DIA at the census date, then discard the census count.
- if a value is still missing, and the person was born after 1985: if not more than 12 months spent outside New Zealand, then assign to the category ‘No children’.
- if not more than 12 months spent outside New Zealand, then assign to the category ‘No children’.
Otherwise, the variable is missing.
Coverage of number of children born in the APC is highest at 86 percent in 2013 when the use of 2013 Census information is most beneficial, and decreases gradually to 83 percent in 2021. Before 2013, coverage is around 50 percent. These missing data patterns are driven by distinct features of the data sources. Before 2013, the low coverage is due to the lack of historical digitised DIA birth registrations for older mothers, however this becomes less important over time.
The lack of information on babies born overseas is an ongoing issue, mainly for new migrants, and affects both coverage and measurement error. Coverage is reduced since we have no information for migrant women when they enter the resident population. For those that have had children born overseas, and subsequently have children in New Zealand, the number of children will be under-counted. This could be partially addressed by more detailed visa data, however there will potentially always be some births missed, for example, because they occur overseas for women not requiring a visa, including New Zealand-born women.
The quality rating score for number of children born alive is derived from the consistency between individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. Consistency is defined as exact agreement of number of children born. The quality rating score in 2018 is 0.95.

We compare distributions between the APC in 2018 and the 2018 Census for Number of children born (figure 5), where the categories for four and more children are combined. This shows overall good agreement. 2018 Census has a larger proportion of ‘No children’, which is possibly due to the lack of positive identification of this category in admin data. This also explains why the other categories tend to be proportionally larger in the APC compared with census.

Ethnicity is a core demographic variable defined in the Ethnicity New Zealand Standard Classification as “the ethnic group or groups a person identifies with or has a sense of belonging to. It is a measure of cultural affiliation (in contrast to race, ancestry, nationality, or citizenship). Ethnicity is self-perceived and a person can belong to more than one ethnic group.”
Ethnicity is a hierarchical classification of four levels. Level 1 of the classification has six categories: European, Māori, Pacific Peoples, Asian; Middle Eastern, Latin American and African (MELAA), and Other. Level 1 is used solely for outputs, not collection. The ‘Other’ category is very small and is combined with European for the official ethnic estimated resident population.
Apart from Māori, level 1 categories are groupings of ethnicities, not ethnicities as such. Level 2 has 21 categories, level 3 has 36 categories, and level 4 has 180 categories, excluding residual categories. Ethnicities with larger populations in New Zealand appear in level 2 while those with smaller populations are only reported as separate ethnicities at level 3 or level 4. For example, for the level 1 Pacific Peoples group, at level 2 Samoan and Tongan are ethnicities, while smaller Pacific ethnicities are coded as “Other Pacific’. Level 4 includes smaller populations such as Hawaiian and Tahitian, as well as the larger Pacific ethnicities. See Ethnicity New Zealand Standard Classification 2005 V2.1.0 for details.
The APC derives ethnicity from multiple administrative sources (DIA birth registrations, MoE schools and MoE tertiary data, MoH, and MSD). 2013 Census ethnicity data is used for 0.5 percent of individuals who have no ethnicity in administrative sources. In a change from the first iteration of the APC, ethnicity information from both the parents DIA birth registrations is used rather than just from the child, leading to an increase in quality. The APC applies the source ranking method described in Reid et al (2016) and refined in Stats NZ (2018a). The administrative data sources have been ranked by quality. The ethnicities provided in the highest quality available source are selected for the individual. If a source provides multiple responses from different dates, the most recent one is used. While a person may change their ethnicity over time, current methods and the lack of time references in some data sources do not allow for this, and therefore ethnicity is treated as constant.
The data sources collect ethnicity information at varying levels from the most detailed level 4 (DIA, 2013 Census), to level 3 (MoE) and level 2 (MoH, MSD). Level 1 ethnic group is coded as six indicator variables. In the first APC release, the ethnicity classification below level 1 was coded as a single variable, and only to the level of detail reported by the highest-ranking source. While this approach retains consistency for an individual at all levels of the classification, it does not make use of the more detailed ethnicity classification that may be available for an individual in lower ranked sources.
The APC release in 2022 modifies the source ranking method to improve the derivation and usability of levels 3 to 4. Levels 2, 3, and 4 are presented as distinct variables. Separate ethnicities reported at level 1, 2, or 3 are carried through the more detailed levels to the level 4 classification.
Each level of the classification is derived separately, selecting the highest ranked source available at that level. Since all sources provide at least level 2 of the classification, the derivation of level 1 and 2 is unchanged.
The main impact is the increased use of 2013 Census data (otherwise the lowest rank source) for the small populations only reported as distinct ethnicities at level 4. While this approach means there could be cases where an individual’s values are contradictory between levels, this rarely happens in practice. The alternatives here would be to only use sources that provide level 4 detail, which would reduce coverage for levels 2 and 3, or discard contradictory information above level 2, which would reduce coverage for levels 3 and 4.
APC ethnicity derivation
Derived from DIA birth registrations (for parents and children), MoE schools, MoE tertiary, MoH, MSD, and the 2013 Census.
Sources are ranked in order: DIA parents > DIA children > MoE tertiary > MoH > MoE schools > MSD > 2013 Census.
For every unique individual (snz_uid) on the IDI spine:
- Compile ethnicity from all sources: if a source provides multiple responses from different dates, use the most recent one derive the more detailed classification levels for separate ethnicities reported at level 1, level 2, and level 3.
- if a source provides multiple responses from different dates, use the most recent one
- derive the more detailed classification levels for separate ethnicities reported at level 1, level 2, and level 3.
- For each level 2, 3, and 4, independently select the highest-ranked source that provides information at that level.
- Derive level 1 ethnic group from level 2 ethnicity.
Otherwise, the variable is missing.
Coverage for level 1 and level 2 ethnicity in the APC is very high at 99.3 percent in 2006 to above 99.6 percent from 2012 on. Level 3 has a coverage rate of 98 percent or higher across the time series. Coverage for level 4 is lower, ranging from 95 percent in 2021 to almost 99 percent in 2013.
Quality measures for level 1 ethnic groups have been derived using the Dempster-Shafer Theory method. Derivation of the input quality of the data sources is described in Appendix 4. Each level 1 ethnic group is an indicator variable (either a person belongs, or does not belong, to that group). We derive a quality rating score for those belonging to that ethnic group. Since missingness for level 1 ethnicity is very low, the output ratings are mostly driven by the non-missing quality rating. The output quality rating scores for each level 1 ethnic group range between 0.92 and 0.96 for European, Māori, Pacific People, and Asian. The scores for the MELAA and Other groups are 0.79 and 0.26 respectively, reflecting known patterns of quality issues.
Table 2 shows three level 1 ethnic groups further broken down by broad age groups, and shows only minor variation among age groups. The patterns are similar for the remaining level 1 ethnic groups that are not included in the table.

Level 1 ethnic group estimates from the APC are compared with the 2018 Census and the official ERP for 2018 (table 3). Overall, the APC exhibits very similar patterns to the census and the official ERP. Differences in counts partially reflect differences in the total population, and level 1 ethnic group as a proportion of the total population may be a better reflection of the accuracy of administrative ethnicity derivation compared with the official ERP estimates. The proportions for each level 1 ethnic group are all within 1 percent.

In 2018, the APC count for Māori is 3.6 percent lower than the ERP, compared with a 1.8 percent undercount for the total population. The age distribution of the APC closely follows the ERP distribution (figure 6) though with some discrepancies in the late teens.
Māori ethnicity usual resident population, for APC and ERP in 2018, by single year age
Figure 7 compares distributions between the APC in 2018 and the 2018 Census for the 15 largest level 3 ethnicities. Proportions are close, with the exception of European ‘not further defined’ which is higher in the APC than the census, and correspondingly lower APC proportion of New Zealand European. Overcounting of ‘not further defined’ and grouped categories such as ‘Other Pacific’ was found to be prevalent in certain agencies (Stats NZ, 2018a). Other anomalies found in the 2018 study were over-reporting of Fijian ethnicity across most agencies because Fijian Indians are being incorrectly coded to Fijian (and therefore Pacific rather than Asian at level 1), and over-reporting of the African ethnic group in some agencies. All these issues are apparent in the APC as well at levels 2, 3 and 4. We note that admin ‘not further defined’ categories have been retained in the APC, and not replaced with 2013 Census responses.
While level 4 counts are still good overall, populations can be very small and there is more variation between the census and APC. There is a general pattern that APC is overcounting ‘not further defined’, and ‘not elsewhere classified’ categories, while undercounting some level 4 ethnicities.
While the use of 2013 Census data has improved the coverage of level 3 and level 4 ethnicities in the APC, this is not a long-term solution. There is a need for improved collection and coding practices of ethnicity by most agencies.

The statistical standard for Māori descent defines the concept as: “A person has Māori descent if they are of the Māori race of New Zealand: this includes any descendant of such a person.” http://aria.stats.govt.nz/aria/
The classification has three response options: ‘Yes’, ‘No’, and ‘Don’t know’.
The Māori descent variable for the APC is derived from DIA birth registrations, and from the 2013 Census. In a change from the first iteration of the APC, DIA death registrations and household labour force survey (HLFS) responses are also used but provide additional information only for a very small number of individuals. All sources follow the standard and include ‘Don’t know’ as a valid response option.
The birth registration form question on Māori descent is asked of the parents as well as the child. We treat the parent’s response as self-reported and the child’s as a proxy response provided by the parents. The same person may have reported Māori descent in several birth registration records: they may have been born in New Zealand, later becoming a parent themselves; or have had more than one child in New Zealand.
The statistical standard states that Māori descent is based on a genealogical or biological concept, and therefore we may assume that it does not change over time. In practice, however, the same person can report different descent values. In the logic of the derivation, we prioritise the most recently reported value within birth registrations (including preferring the self-reported value of a parent, over the proxy response of their own birth registration).
APC Māori descent derivation
Derived from DIA birth registrations (for parents and children), the 2013 Census, DIA death registrations, and HLFS responses.
For every unique individual (snz_uid) on the IDI spine:
- Valid responses from administrative sources (births) are used first. Births data can have multiple information for the same snz_uid; in case of conflicting information: self-response (that is, parent record) is prioritised over a proxy response (that is, child record) if there are multiple values for the same person, we use the most recent one.
- Births data can have multiple information for the same snz_uid; in case of conflicting information: self-response (that is, parent record) is prioritised over a proxy response (that is, child record) if there are multiple values for the same person, we use the most recent one.
- self-response (that is, parent record) is prioritised over a proxy response (that is, child record)
- if there are multiple values for the same person, we use the most recent one.
- If a value is still missing, a valid 2013 Census response is used where available, followed by DIA death registrations and HLFS responses,
- followed by DIA death registrations and HLFS responses,
Otherwise, the variable is missing.
For Māori descent, ‘yes’, ‘no’ and ‘don’t know’ are all valid answers.
In the current APC data, coverage of Māori descent peaks at 91 percent in 2013 and decreases gradually both before and after 2013, down to 84 percent in 2021. Missing data is partly due to the lack of administrative sources before 1995 when Māori descent was first collected in birth registrations. The use of 2013 Census data significantly improves coverage. For the 2013 year, the rate of missing data in the APC (9 percent) is slightly better than rate of ‘not elsewhere included’ in the 2013 Census (10 percent) ( Stats NZ Datainfo+ ). Coverage of the New Zealand-born population will gradually increase over time. Some migrants will be captured in the DIA births data, if they have children born in New Zealand, but missing Māori descent for migrants will continue to grow as we move further away from 2013. Given the importance of Māori descent information, this level of missing data is unlikely to be sufficient to derive estimates of Māori descent of the quality required for electoral calculations, for example.
In this iteration of the APC, we have included Māori descent from death registrations and the household labour force survey (HLFS). Death registrations improve the coverage of earlier years for older ages, and while the overall impact is small, it does provide a more consistent time series. The HLFS has a negligible effect on coverage but provides more information for the quality metric calculations.
Quality measures for Māori descent have been derived using Dempster-Shafer Theory. The quality rating score for non-missing data is 0.95, and with 14 percent missing data in 2018, the output overall output quality rating is 0.82. Table 4 shows how the quality measures vary by birthplace. The differences are mainly due to the level of missing data rather than any differences in the quality of the administrative sources.

Table 5 shows the counts for Māori descent in the APC and the 2018 Census, and the percent distribution of Māori descent calculated from the stated values. ### Missing Māori descent data
Figure 8 shows the proportion of missing values for Māori descent by age group for New Zealand-born, and overseas-born. The patterns are quite different. For those born in New Zealand, those over 65 years have the highest levels of missing data, and this stabilises in 2013. However, for those born overseas, from 2013 onwards the rate of missing data increases every year. This is especially true for younger age groups, reaching 80 percent missing for children aged 0–14 in 2020. The overseas-born population has very low rates of people with Māori descent. Of people stating they were of Māori descent, in the 2013 Census 2.1 percent were born overseas, and in the 2018 Census 2.4 percent were born overseas. Therefore, we can expect that most of those with missing data who were born overseas will report Māori descent = No, while those born in New Zealand will be a mix of responses.

In summary, with the sources currently available for the APC, undercoverage of Māori descent is significant, it varies by age, is higher for those born overseas, and is increasing over time due to the lack of sources for many migrants to New Zealand.
The electoral roll also collects Māori descent and the combination of birth registrations and electoral roll data (those 18 years and older who enrol to vote) would provide high coverage for much of the population on an ongoing basis. The recent passing of the Data and Statistics Act enables Stats NZ to access electoral data, and research will be a priority once data is available. Even with additional sources, imputation of remaining missing values will be needed – as is the case with the census now. The patterns of missing data are likely to be different from those usually seen in census non-response. Those missing from the administrative data are most likely to be migrants under the age of 18 years, and migrant adults and older New Zealand-born people who do not enrol to vote.

Birthplace is the country where a person was born.
There are two standard country classifications in use: the four-numeric classification and the two-alpha classification. Stats NZ’s standard country classifications are aligned to the countries recognised by the United Nations Statistical Division and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO). The four-numeric classification includes supplementary codes used to process inadequate responses, for example, ‘North-East Asia – not further defined (nfd).’ See Statistical standard for country V1.0.0 for details.
Birthplace is derived from DIA birth registration records for those born in New Zealand, and birth country from MBIE passport and visa information for those born overseas. 2013 Census is used to fill in gaps where administrative data is missing.
The 2013 Census codes responses to the four-numeric classification using the name of the country at the time of the census.
MBIE data is coded according to the two-alpha classification. The classification will reflect the country name at the time at which the passport or visa was issued. MBIE codes were converted to the four-numeric country classification at the time of the 2018 Census.
The United Kingdom presents a special case. The four-numeric codes used by the census include countries within the United Kingdom as well as the United Kingdom (nfd). While individual countries in the United Kingdom appear in the two-alpha classification, only the broader United Kingdom exists as a birthplace in the MBIE data.

## Years since arrival in NZ
Year of arrival in New Zealand is the year that a person who was born outside of New Zealand first arrived in New Zealand as a permanent or long-term resident. The years since arrival is derived by measuring the time elapsed (in completed years) between the first year of arrival in New Zealand and the reference date, irrespective of any intervening absences, whether temporary or long term ( Statistical standard for years since arrival in New Zealand ).
The administrative data source is the Customs border movements data which starts in September 1997. The date of border crossing for the first year of arrival of a migrant has been determined by Stats NZ using the 12/16 migration definition. The number of years since arrival in New Zealand is derived from the date of arrival the first time the migrant achieved resident status according to the 12/16-month rule.
2013 Census is used to fill gaps where administrative data is missing. The 2013 Census is the only available source for migrants arriving in New Zealand prior to 1997. Use of 2013 Census data for those who arrived before 1997 also avoids incorrectly using more recent border movements as their date of first arrival. For arrivals between 1997 and 2000 we prioritise 2013 Census data and use an arrival date derived from the 12/16-month rule if no 2013 Census data is available.
Our confidence in the migration estimates derived using the 12/16-month rule is higher after 2000 as it is possible to compare it with the intercensal migration estimate using 2001 and 2006 Census data and good agreement is observed (Stats NZ, 2017). Therefore, the Customs border movement data is our highest-ranking source for migrant arrivals after 2000.
In cases where the 2013 Census month of arrival is missing, we randomly impute a month, consistent with the derived years since arrival in the 2013 Census.
Two edits are applied to ensure consistency within the APC dataset. The first edit is to ensure that the date of arrival in New Zealand is later than the date of birth. If not, date of arrival is set to missing. The second edit checks that date of arrival coincides with or is before first inclusion in the APC. If not, date of arrival is set to missing.
For those born in New Zealand, years since arrival in New Zealand is not applicable.
APC Years since arrival in New Zealand derivation
Derived from Customs border movements and 2013 Census.
For every unique individual snz_uid on the IDI spine with an overseas-born indicator:
- if date of arrival in 2013 Census is earlier than 2000, this month and year of arrival is used
- if a 12/16 migration indicator exists, the date of first arrival is the border crossing date of the earliest migration status change from overseas visitor to resident
- if a value is still missing, 2013 Census month and year of arrival is used where available
- otherwise the year of arrival is missing.
The date of arrival must be later than date of birth and must coincide with or be before the date of first inclusion in the APC.
Years since arrival is calculated as the number of completed years from the date of arrival to 30 June of each year of the APC.
Years since arrival is not applicable for New Zealand-born.
Coverage for years since arrival in New Zealand is very high at 97 percent from 2013 onwards. Missing data is largely due to the lack of administrative information for migrants to New Zealand before 1997, when electronic border movements data was first available, affecting those who were not included in the 2013 Census. Coverage will gradually tend towards 100 percent over time since the border movements data includes almost all new migrants to New Zealand.
Quality measures for years since arrival in New Zealand have been derived using the Dempster-Shafer method. To account for the limitations of census responses, the input quality rating for years since arrival in New Zealand is calculated as being agreement within one year. The quality rating score for non-missing data is 0.93, and with 4 percent missing data in 2018, the output overall output quality rating is 0.89. There is some variation in rating scores by birthplace. Non-missing quality rating scores remain high, and differences in the output quality rating reflect differing levels of missing data (table 8).

## Highest qualifications and field of study
The education data provides positive identification of formal qualifications awarded by recognised New Zealand authorities. Some information for those with no qualifications is available on enrolment in targeted training courses.
Highest qualification is the highest secondary school or post-school qualification by category of attainment. A secondary school and post-school qualification is assigned to one of the 10 levels of the New Zealand Qualifications Framework (NZQF). The census highest qualifications classification also includes a category for ‘No qualification’. Another census category ‘Overseas Secondary School Qualification’ has not been populated in the APC, although this information is available in the 2013 Census and could be included in future. Unlike the census, in the APC we do not provide separate variables for the highest secondary school qualification, and highest post-school qualification. Highest qualification is derived for people aged 15 years and over.
Qualification subject is the main topic or field of study of a post-school qualification. The subject population for field of study in the APC is those 15 years and over with a highest post-school qualification. Field of Study is classified using the New Zealand Standard Classification of Education - Field of Study V2.0.0 . The APC provides field of study codes to the lowest (most detailed) level of the classification (level 3, with 375 categories). The census uses a different subject population of all those aged 15 years and over, and the census classification also includes categories for ‘No post-school qualification’, and ‘Field of study not given (although level of attainment given)’.
Highest qualification and field of study are derived from Ministry of Education schools, tertiary, industry training organisations, and targeted training datasets, and the 2013 Census. Most qualifications come from qualifications awarded by providers; however, some information (including no qualifications) are self-reported on enrolment in courses. Tertiary institution qualifications are available from 1994, NZQA qualifications (which includes all school qualifications) from 2007, industry training from 2003, and targeted training from 2001. Where a post-school qualification is obtained for multiple fields of study, all are included in the APC.
The 2013 Census data provides information for those whose qualifications were gained before digitisation of education datasets, as well as those with no qualifications in 2013, or qualifications gained overseas. However, the 2013 Census data only gives the highest qualification at the date of the census, and does not state when the qualification was obtained. Therefore all 2013 Census qualifications are given the attainment date of 6 March 2013. Only one field of study can be reported in the census.
Information for each qualification gained by an individual is recorded: details include the level achieved, the date attained, and fields of study (where applicable). A concordance to the standard NZQF classification is applied as needed. These qualifications are combined across all sources to create a record of qualifications gained over time for each individual. The highest qualification attained at 30 June of the reference year is selected.
APC highest qualifications and field of study derivation
Derived from Ministry of Education data for secondary schools, tertiary education institutions, industry training organisations, and targeted training programmes; and the 2013 Census.
Using all available sources, a record of qualifications gained over time is compiled for each individual appearing in the Ministry of Education datasets and the 2013 Census.
For every unique individual (snz_uid) in the APC in the reference year:
- Select the highest qualification gained up to 30 June of the reference year
- For those with a post-school highest qualification, all fields of study are recorded
- ‘No qualifications’ is derived from the 2013 Census, Government funded industry training, industry training programs, and tertiary qualification enrolments.
- Otherwise, highest qualification is missing.

### Results for highest qualifications
Coverage of highest qualifications peaks at 84 percent coverage in 2013 and decreases gradually after, down to 82 percent in 2018. Coverage before 2013 is low (dropping to 55 percent in 2012 and down to 40 percent in 2006) because the 2013 Census has no information on when a qualification was obtained.
While the 2013 Census data significantly improves coverage from 2013 onwards, the relatively high level of missing data in the 2013 Census (11.1 percent highest qualification ‘not elsewhere included’ Stats NZ DataInfo+ ) limits the information available for qualifications gained prior to digitisation. Coverage will continue to slowly decrease from 2013 onwards because there is limited information available for qualifications obtained overseas, mainly affecting new migrants. This could be partially addressed by more detailed visa data, however there will potentially always be some qualifications missed because they are gained overseas.
Those who enter the subject population when they reach 15 years of age with no qualifications are another group contributing to missing data after 2013. An approach to resolving these cases would be to classify those aged 15 years as having ‘No qualifications’. This would remain valid until either they gain a qualification in New Zealand, or until they leave New Zealand for a certain period of time, such as one year, when a qualification might be gained overseas.
The quality rating score for highest qualification is derived by comparing the consistency of highest qualification between individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. We allow for misreporting in census and assume that a higher APC attainment level is more accurate than a lower census response. Consistency for highest qualification was defined as the individual’s APC qualification level being equal to or higher than the census value. The quality rating score for highest qualifications is 0.86.

### Results for post-school qualifications field of study
Coverage is calculated as the percent of those with post-school qualifications who have a non-missing value of field of study. Coverage for APC field of study in 2018 is 87 percent, which is similar to 2013 when coverage was 88 percent.
We note that the category ‘Field of study not given (although level of attainment given)’ is treated as missing field of study in the APC, whereas the census includes these among the ‘stated’ category.
Coverage of field of study is generally good for administrative sources because tertiary and industry training organisations are required to provide field of study for qualifications awarded. The majority of the missing data for field of study comes from targeted training where field of study is not available.
Some post-school qualifications may be missing, for example because MoE data does not include qualifications gained overseas. This could lead to missing data for field of study (if all post-school qualifications were gained overseas) or measurement error if the qualifications gained in New Zealand are superseded by higher post-school qualifications or a different field of study gained overseas. However, the proportion of post-school qualifications among those with missing highest qualifications cannot be determined.
The quality rating score for field of study is derived by comparing the consistency of field of study between individuals with valid values (categories 1 to 11) in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. Field of study consistency was defined as the census field of study value being represented at least once in any of the APC admin field of study values, regardless of the year in which the qualification was obtained. Consistency for field of study was 0.83 at the broad level 1 categories.
We note that this definition may be a conservative estimate of the quality of the admin sources, since the MoE provides the formal recognition of a qualification and the field of study from a registered record of a qualification completed through an approved provider. However, for the census we rely on the respondent’s interpretation of both their highest post-school qualification and the main field of study, and the coding of a text response to field of study.
The distribution of field of study broad level categories for 2018 are shown in figure 13 for the APC in 2018 and the 2018 Census. Both sources have a similar ranking of the most common subjects and the least common. Proportions in the categories of broad field of study differ by between 0 and 3 percent between census and the APC. This could be due to factors such as: differing patterns of missing qualifications data between the two sources, some individuals reporting a different field of study in census because it was gained overseas; the nature of questionnaire responses which are reliant on the respondent’s interpretation and ability to code text responses; the census accepting only one field of study, whereas the APC can have multiple fields.

Participation in education or training measures those attending, studying, or enrolled in tertiary institutions, school, early childhood education, or any other place of education or training. It is grouped into full-time study (20 hours or more a week), part-time study (less than 20 hours a week), and those not studying. The APC defines ‘study participation’ for those 15 years and older.
For the APC, study participation is derived from Ministry of Education data for secondary school enrolments, and enrolment in courses at tertiary education institutions, industry training organisations, and targeted training programmes. Enrolment in school or a tertiary course over a period that includes the reference date of 30 June indicates participation in study. Otherwise, the person is defined as not studying. Enrolment in secondary school is assumed to be full-time study. Tertiary education institutions provide information on whether study is full-time or part-time. Enrolment in industry training is assumed to be part-time and targeted training is unknown and coded as studying, not further defined.
Since MoE data provides very high coverage of New Zealand educational institutions, we assume that anyone not enrolled is not studying. Study participation is available in the APC from 2007, the first year of school roll returns.

## Personal income: Total personal income and income sources
‘Total personal income’ is defined as the total before-tax income an individual aged 15 years and over receives for a financial year from all sources. ‘Sources of personal income’ identifies the various sources from which a person received income during the financial year. The Census Sources of Personal Income classification V3.0.0 identifies twelve sources of income. The classification also includes a category for ‘No source of income during that time’.
In the census, total personal income is collected as income bands rather than in actual dollars, and income sources are asked as a separate question. In the APC, income is calculated as the dollar amount for each source, and total personal income is the sum across all income sources. Income for the APC reference year is the total income obtained during the financial year ended 31 March of the reference year.
Tax and benefit income source codes are recoded to match the income sources classification. For example, taxable rental income is included in category 03 ‘Interest, dividends, rent, other investments’, and paid parental leave is coded as category 11 ‘Other government benefits, government income support payments, war pensions, or paid parental leave’.
Personal income is derived from IR tax data tables in the IDI, MSD benefits payments, and Working for Families (WFF) data. The IDI collates annual income for the financial year from several IR sources (Employee Monthly Schedule (EMS), and IR7, IR4S, and IR3 annual tax forms). This IDI-derived summary is taken as the authoritative source for income from wages and salaries, ACC payments, student allowances and self-employment, including company director/shareholder income, partnership income, and sole trader income.
MSD is the preferred source for income from benefits and NZ Superannuation, with IR data acting as a fallback if needed. Investment income data is taken from a variety of IR data sources: IR3, Personal Tax Summary (PTS), and Automatic Assessment (AA), and the IDI-derived tax year income data. WFF provides child support payments and income tax credits.
All sources of income listed in the classification are available to some degree within the admin data sources, except for category 06 (Other superannuation, pensions, or annuities). However, there is little information available in these sources to directly identify those with no sources of income.

- Use the IR3 data to obtain income data not already included within the IDI-derived table for: interest and dividends, and some ‘other income’ sources of income from estate trust income, self-declared overseas income and self-declared other income.

- Use the PTS and AA to obtain income data not already included within the IDI-derived table for: interest and dividends, Māori authority distribution income (coded as 12 ‘Other income’).
- interest and dividends, Māori authority distribution income (coded as 12 ‘Other income’).
- Combine income sources from all IR tables reconcile any double counting from IR3, PTS and AA tables with priority ordering: IR3 first, then AA, then PTS.
- reconcile any double counting from IR3, PTS and AA tables with priority ordering: IR3 first, then AA, then PTS.
2. Derive income and income sources from MSD and WFF data
- Sum ‘first tier’ taxable benefits from daily gross amounts within the tax year, and code to the appropriate benefit category of the classification. MSD first tier benefits take priority over IR data (the IR benefit data is discarded).
- Sum ‘second tier’ non-taxable supplementary benefit amounts paid to individuals within the tax year, and code to 11 ‘other government benefits’.
- Sum ‘Third tier’ non-recoverable ad hoc payments (loans are excluded), and code to 11 ‘other government benefits’.
- WFF data provides total tax credit income, coded as 11 ‘other government benefit’, and child support income (coded as 12 ‘other income’).
3. Derive total personal income and income sources
- Total personal income is the sum of the income from each of the available income sources.
- Include an income source for an individual if there is a non-zero amount of income calculated from the data.
Coverage for the income variables is consistently high, at around 90 percent prior to 2019 and increasing to around 94 percent from 2019 with the introduction of the IRD automated assessment. Missing data is primarily due to a lack of ability to determine if a person has a true zero income for a given year. IRD has records on inactive IRD numbers which would help to identify individuals with zero income for a given year, and consequently allow for determination of individuals with no source of income. Without an additional data source for those with zero income in a given year, coverage will remain at about 94 percent. This is consistent with the 6 percent of people who reported no source of income in the 2018 Census.
The quality rating score for total personal income is derived by comparing the consistency of income bands between individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. We allow for misreporting in census around the boundaries of income bands and assume that higher tax-derived incomes are more accurate than census responses. Consistency is defined as an individual’s APC income band being within one band of that reported in the census, or two or more bands higher. The quality rating score for total personal income is 0.90.
The quality rating score for sources of personal income is derived by comparing the consistency of income source for individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC data. Consistency is defined as the APC having an income source that was reported in the 2018 Census, where the comparison is made separately within each income source category. The quality rating score for sources of personal income combined across all sources is 0.88. This is dominated by the high consistency within the wages and salary (0.96) and New Zealand Superannuation (0.99) income sources. Investment income has consistency of 0.64 which is expected to increase with future census results as the automated assessment data from IRD was only introduced in 2019. Another lower consistency source is student allowance (0.67), which could be attributed to respondent misunderstanding or failure to distinguish between student loan and student allowance.
Some differences between the census and the APC may be due to a mismatch between the census respondent’s view of what their income and income sources are and that derived from administration data. For example, an individual may not recall a short period of ACC payment they received earlier in the time period covered by the census question.

### Total personal income distributions
We compare the distribution of total personal income, by census income bands, for the APC in 2018, and the 2018 Census (figure 14). The reference period for both sources is the tax year ending 31 March 2018. The APC income distribution is skewed towards higher income bands than are reported in the census. The APC has higher proportions than census in almost all income bands above $20,000, and lower proportions than census in income bands below $20,000.
Zero income is an obvious difference since the tax data we have available does not generally include those with no income. The majority of the `missing` values in the APC will therefore be those individuals with zero income. The $10,001 – $15,000 and $15,001 – $20,000 bands reflect annual government superannuation after-tax and before-tax income respectively in 2018. The 2018 Census has a markedly higher proportion in the $10,001 – $15,000 band, which may reflect some respondents mistakenly reporting their income after tax.
Income loss is also lower in the APC than in the 2018 Census. This could be improved by including additional sources of investment income, such as portfolio investment entity (PIE) sources, as investment income is a common cause of loss over a year.
Distribution of income bands, for the APC in 2018 and 2018 Census

### Sources of personal income distributions
Individuals can have more than one income source, and data is published as the total responses in each category. Figure R compares the distribution of income sources for the APC in 2018, and the 2018 Census. The census questionnaire includes detailed categories for the main types of working-age benefits applicable in 2018, and an ‘Other benefits’ category. The MSD data available for the APC includes details of ‘Other’ benefit categories which are less likely to be reported separately in the census if they are not apparent to the recipient. For example, an individual receiving Jobseeker support may also qualify for the accommodation supplement and winter energy payment. These payments are coded as `Other benefits` within the APC, but may not be distinguished as such by the individual completing the census.
The distribution of sources of income in figure 15 shows good agreement between the census and APC for the largest income source categories. ‘No income’ is the obvious exception because the admin sources available do not directly identify those with no income. Similarly, the APC is unable to identify any ‘Other superannuation’ sources of income as these are usually non-taxable.
The APC also includes a larger proportion within the ‘Other income’ category than the census. This may be partly due to differences between the definitions used within the tax system and how these income streams are perceived by the respondent.

## Employment indicator and Status in employment
The International Labour Organisation (ILO) defines a person as employed if they:
- worked for one hour or more for pay or profit in the context of an employee/employer relationship or self-employment.
- worked without pay for one hour or more in work which contributed directly to the operation of a farm, business, or professional practice owned or operated by a relative.
- had a job but were not at work due to: own illness or injury; personal or family responsibilities; bad weather or mechanical breakdown; direct involvement in industrial dispute; leave, or holiday.
The APC ‘employment indicator’ classifies people aged 15 years and over according to whether they were employed or not. It has two categories: Employed and Not employed. The employment indicator is not the full labour force status classification as it does not classify a person who was not employed as either 'unemployed' or 'not in the labour force'. The APC employment indicator also does not distinguish between full-time or part-time employment for an employed person.
The statistical standard for ‘status in employment’ classifies employed people aged 15 years and over according to whether they were working for themselves or for other people. APC status in employment is classified by paid employees and self-employed. The self-employed are not further classified as ‘employer’, and ‘self-employed without employees’. There is no admin information available for the unpaid family workers category.
Employment indicator and status in employment are derived from IR data: Employee Monthly Schedule (EMS) and annual filings of IR3, IR4S, and IR7. IR data provides high coverage of those in paid employment as everyone is required to pay tax on earned income. There are no sources of information for those who work without pay on a farm, business, or professional practice owned or operated by a relative. It should be noted that tax filing has changed over this period. From April 2019, the EMS was replaced by pay period filing, which is rolled up by Stats NZ to the same monthly format as the EMS.
The census and household labour force survey use a weekly reference period. In the APC, the frequency of filing tax returns dictates the period of employment for the APC reference date of 30 June. For those earning wages and salaries, the EMS provides evidence of employment during the month of June. For those who file annual tax returns, employment in June is assumed if a return has been received for the financial year ended 31 March of the APC year.
Status in employment is determined by the tax filing codes. Paid employees are employed people with income in the EMS that has PAYE deducted. Self-employees are those who file an annual tax return (IR3, IR4S, IR7), or schedular (contractor) payments reported in the EMS. The APC employment indicator gives precedence to income from work if a person has multiple income sources.
Our current method misclassifies some EMS wage and salary payments which are correctly identified as self-employed in the IDI derived tax tables. The next iteration of the APC will correct this.
The APC records all the employment information from the IR tax filing for a person, not just for their main job as asked for in the census. If a person has tax returns indicating they are both self-employed and a paid employee, paid employee takes priority.
APC Employment indicator and Status in employment derivation
Derived from Inland Revenue data
For every unique individual (snz_uid) in the APC reference year aged 15 years and over:
Employment indicator
A person is employed if:
- they receive wages and salaries or schedular payments in June, or
- are receiving wages and salaries or schedular payments in May or July (but not June) for the same employer, or
- have filed IR3, IR4S, or IR7 returns in the tax year ending 31 March of the APC reference year.
Otherwise, they are ‘not employed’.
Status in employment
For every employed person in the APC reference year, a person is classified as:
- a paid employee, if receiving wages and salaries in the EMS
- self-employed, if they have filed IR3/IR4S/IR7 returns in the tax year ending 31 March of the APC reference year or receive schedular payments.
Note: If a person has tax returns indicating they are both self-employed and a paid employee, paid employee takes priority.
The employment indicator in the APC has 100 percent coverage by definition. We assume very high coverage of employment within the tax data because all taxable income is required to be reported to IR. All those not identified as ‘employed’ are assigned as ‘not employed’. Coverage for the status in employment is also 100 percent, since all those identified as employed through taxable earnings can be assigned to either paid employees or self-employed.
The quality rating score for the employment indicator and status in employment is derived by comparing the consistency between individuals with valid values in both 2018 Census responses and APC. Consistency is defined as exact agreement of the employment indicator. The quality rating score for the employment indicator is 0.85, and for status in employment the rating score is 0.92. Some differences between the census and the APC may be due to a mismatch between census respondent’s view of whether they are employed and that derived from taxable earnings. For example, a retired person earning small amounts of income from part-time work may not consider themselves as employed.

Table 10 compares the distribution by employment indicator in the APC and work and labour force status in the 2018 Census, and table 11 shows the status in employment distribution for the 2018 APC and the 2018 Census.
The employed rate is higher in the APC (69.9 percent) than the census (64.7 percent, including 63.8 percent paid employment and 0.9 percent unpaid family workers). This compares with the employment rate of 67.7 percent for both the March and June quarters as measured by the household labour force survey .
The APC has correspondingly lower proportions for not employed (30.1 percent) than the census (35.3 percent, including 4.0 percent for unemployed and 31.3 percent for not in the labour force).
Status in employment counts for paid employees and self-employed in the APC are about 13 percent higher than those of the census, reflecting the higher number of employed. The proportion of paid employees and self-employed are respectively 76.7 percent and 23.3 percent in the APC. In comparison, the paid employee proportion is higher (83.9 percent) in the census, and the self-employment proportion is lower (16.1 percent, including 5.7 percent employer and 10.4 percent self-employed and without employees).
The APC assigns a lower proportion of the paid employees than the census. This may be partially attributed to the fact that: (i) the APC assumes a person who has filed IR3/IR4S/IR7 returns in the tax year ending 31 March of the APC reference year as self-employed at reference date 30 June while they are possibly not in the job market,or (ii) the census classifies status in employment for the employed person’s main job, while the APC does not identify people’s main job. If a person’s tax returns indicate both self-employed and paid employee, paid employee takes priority.
Some of the differences between census and admin sources may be due to misalignment between census respondents’ interpretation of employment compared with their taxable earnings. Similarly, a person’s interpretation of whether they are self-employed may differ from how self-employment is defined within the tax system.

## Industry and sector of ownership
Industry is the type of activity undertaken by the organisation, enterprise, business, or unit of economic activity in which the individual was employed. Business Register industry codes use the Australian and New Zealand Standard Industrial Classification 2006 (ANZSIC 2006).
Sector of ownership for the organisation identifies the part of the economy that owns an organisation, enterprise, business, or unit of economic activity in which the individual was employed. Business Register sector of ownership uses the New Zealand Standard Institutional Sector Classification 1996V1.0.0.
Industry and sector of ownership for the business a person is employed in are derived from the Business Register. The Business Register derives industry for at the enterprise-level on the basis of the predominant activity of the enterprise. The tax files provide the IRD number for the employer or self-employed business, and this IRD number is used to link to the Business Register. Smaller businesses with GST turnover less than $30,000 per year are not included on the Business Register, and will not have an industry or sector of ownership code in the APC.
Industry coding in the APC is based on enterprises. In contrast, the census uses the industry code of geographical locations (geos), by matching the employer‘s name and workplace address provided by the respondent to the Business Register. For some enterprises consisting of multiple geos, there may be geos with a different industry code from the enterprise.
The APC records all the industry and sector of ownership information from the IR tax filing for a person, not just for their main job as in the census.
APC Industry and sector of ownership derivation
Derived from Inland Revenue data and the Business Register
For every employed person in the APC reference year:
- use the employer IRD number from the IR tax filing to link to the Business Register
- select the industry and sector of ownership of the employer’s enterprise on the Business Register.
Note: The reference date month should be between the first and last month in which the enterprise variables hold in the Business Register.
Coverage for industry and sector of ownership are around 80 percent. Sector of ownership has slightly higher coverage than industry. Missing data is primarily due to low coverage for the self-employed people who have filed annual returns, and are below the threshold for inclusion in the Business Register. Business Industry Codes used for ACC purposes are included in the Inland Revenue data and are a potential source of industry for these smaller businesses. Smaller business for self-employed are most likely to belong to the private sector.
Since the Business Register provides the authoritative classification of industry and sector of ownership for the enterprise, the quality rating score in the APC is 1 for both variables.
We can also compare the consistency of the APC values derived from the Business Register with census responses, between individuals with valid values in both the 2018 Census and APC.
Differences may be attributed to the following:
- A person may have multiple jobs and file tax returns from EMS/IR3/IR4S/IR7. The APC records all the industry and sector of ownership information from the IR tax filing for a person. The census only records the industry and sector of ownership information of a person’s main job.
- The APC uses the industry of the enterprise, while the census uses the industry of part of the business (Geographic location, ‘ Geo’). Some larger businesses may include Geo’s in a different industry from the main enterprise.
- Those employed by businesses specialising in providing temporary labour (‘temps’) will be coded in the Business Register to the industry of the labour supply business, while a census respondent may provide the industry of the business they are temping for.
- Census responses may be misclassified, perhaps because the respondent is not able to provide enough information, or due to errors in processing.
To allow for multiple jobs in the APC, consistency for industry has been calculated as whether the APC includes the census response at the top level industry classification. Similarly, consistency for sector of ownership is calculated as whether the APC records includes the census response at the census sector of ownership classification. Consistency between the 2018 Census response and APC for industry is 0.79, and 0.95 for sector of ownership.
